text: The problem is to find, in a set of $N \ = \ 2 ^ { n }$ elements, a subset of M elements that satisfies some given conditions. We say then that the search problem has M solutions.
We can assume the existence of a function $f ( x )$ from n bits to 1 bit, that takes the value $f ( x ) = 1$ if x is a solution and $f ( x ) = 0$ if x is not a solution.

We also assume that there is a black box (an “oracle”) $U _ { f }$ able to execute the operation on the $n + 1$ qubit state $| x \rangle | q \rangle$ , where $| x \rangle$ is a n-qubit state and $| q \rangle$ is a 1-qubit state:
In this example $N = 4$ , and the oracle that tests x is one of the four gates:

image_caption: Fig. 24.4 Oracles for the four cases: solution $x _ { 0 } = 0 , \ldots$ solution $x _ { 0 } = 3$ .

text: each corresponding to a particular solution $x _ { 0 } .$
The first two qubits (the “query” qubits) encode $x ,$ the last qubit is the oracle response. The circuit for the search of the solution is:

image_caption: Fig. 24.5 circuit for the search of the solution $x _ { 0 }$
content:
```mermaid
graph LR
  H1["H"] --> Oracle["Oracle"]
  H2["H"] --> Oracle
  H3["H"] --> Oracle
  Oracle --> H1
  Oracle --> H2
  Oracle --> H3
  H1 --> X["X"]
  H2 --> X
  H3 --> X
  X --> H1
  X --> H2
  H1 --> X
  H2 --> H1
  H1 --> X
  H2 --> X
  X --> H1
  X --> H2
  H1 --> X
  H2 --> X
  X --> H1
  H1 --> H2
  H2 --> H1
  X --> H2
  H1 --> H1
  H2 --> H2
  H1 --> H1
  H2 --> H2
```

text: Initially, the two query qubits are in the state <sub>|</sub>00<sub>i</sub>, and the last qubit in the state <sub>|</sub>1<sub>i</sub>. Only one iteration is required to obtain exactly $x _ { 0 }$ . One can verify, using the circuit, that the measurement of the top two qubits gives $x _ { 0 }$ after using the oracle only once.
By contrast, a classical computer trying to find $x _ { 0 }$ by examining all the two-bit x (four of them) would require an average of 2.25 oracle calls.
